coredata.dc:title: Electronic structure modulation in quinoline derivatives through substituent-mediated effects: Development of AIE fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection in water samples

Zhang, Yuchen
Chen, Yibo
Huang, Xiangdi

Zheng, Liyan
Cao, Qiue
coredata.dc:description: Iron (Fe3+) is an essential trace element in biological organisms. Abnormal concentrations of Fe3+ in aquatic possess the potential to disrupt normal physiological and metabolic processes in living organisms. Consequently, the pursuit of developing fluorescent probes for detecting Fe3+ concentrations in water samples is highly significant. The lone electron pair on the nitrogen atom of quinoline demonstrates outstanding coordination capabilities, enabling the selective detection of metal ions through coordination. Nevertheless, the interaction between metal ions and quinoline-based fluorescent probes tends to lead to nanoparticle aggregation, causing aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomena. This severely restricts the practical application of these probes. To address this challenge, the study utilizes quinoline as the foundational framework and modulates the excited-state electronic structure of quinoline derivatives through substituent effects. This facilitates the transition from ACQ to aggregation-induced emission (AIE). By integrating theoretical calculations, the paper proposes a comprehensive strategy for designing AIE molecules based on quinoline. This contribution provides innovative perspectives on AIE molecule design. Ultimately, the AIE property of the 8-MQB molecule is harnessed to develop a fluorescent probe capable of detecting Fe3+ in water samples. The fluorescence intensity exhibits a robust linear correlation with Fe3+ concentrations within the range of 5.0 μM to 0.3 mM. Moreover, the probe demonstrates exceptional resistance to interference from other metal ions. In conclusion, this research presents a universal strategy for designing AIE molecules and introduces an AIE probe for the rapid detection of Fe3+ concentrations in water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quinoline
Aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ)
Aggregation-induced emission (AIE)
Excited-state electronic structure
Detection of Fe3+

The photophysical properties of quinoline derivatives in solid state
Model creation for AIE molecular design

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Left: 8-QBA (A), 8-MQB (B), 8-QPA (C), and 8-MPQ(D) natural transition orbitals of S1–S0; Right: Energy of T1,min and S1,min SOC, and k
risc date.

Left: The crystal structure of 8-QBA (A), 8-MQB (B), 8-QPA (C), and 8-MPQ (D). O atoms (red), N atoms (blue), C atoms (white), and H atoms (white). Middle: a view of the Hirshfeld surface mapped over d
norm for the compounds. Right: The pie charts that showing the relative contributions of various intermolecular interactions to the Hirshfeld surface area.
ESP and EDM values of 8-QBA, 8-MQB, 8-QPA and 8-MPQ.

(A) 8-QBZ and 8-QLP natural transition orbitals of S1–S0. (B) ESP and EDM values of 8-QBZ and 8-QLP. (C) and (D) (Left) PL spectra of 8-QBZ (up) and 8-QLP (down) in water/THF mixtures with different water fractions (f
w ). The concentrations of these molecules were 10−5 M. (Middle): The crystal structure of compounds. O atoms (red), N atoms (blue), C atoms (white), and H atoms (white). Right: a view of the Hirshfeld surface mapped over d norm for the compound and the pie charts showing the relative contributions of various intermolecular interactions to the Hirshfeld surface area.

(A) Response selectivity of the 8-MQB to different metal ions. (B) Anti-interference performance of 8-MQB response to Fe3+. (C) PL spectra of 8-MQB with Fe3+ different concentrations. (D) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of 8-QMB and concentrations of Fe3+ ions.

A. Previous studies exploring AIE Molecule Design Through (n, π*) Transitions. Adapted with permission from Refs. [20,22]. Copyright 2019 Wiley-VCH and 2023 Nature Communications. B. Scheme of this work.

head.ce:title: Electronic structure modulation in quinoline derivatives through substituent-mediated effects: Development of AIE fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection in water samples

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iron (Fe3+) is an essential trace element in biological organisms. Abnormal concentrations of Fe3+ in aquatic possess the potential to disrupt normal physiological and metabolic processes in living organisms. Consequently, the pursuit of developing fluorescent probes for detecting Fe3+ concentrations in water samples is highly significant. The lone electron pair on the nitrogen atom of quinoline demonstrates outstanding coordination capabilities, enabling the selective detection of metal ions through coordination. Nevertheless, the interaction between metal ions and quinoline-based fluorescent probes tends to lead to nanoparticle aggregation, causing aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomena. This severely restricts the practical application of these probes. To address this challenge, the study utilizes quinoline as the foundational framework and modulates the excited-state electronic structure of quinoline derivatives through substituent effects. This facilitates the transition from ACQ to aggregation-induced emission (AIE). By integrating theoretical calculations, the paper proposes a comprehensive strategy for designing AIE molecules based on quinoline. This contribution provides innovative perspectives on AIE molecule design. Ultimately, the AIE property of the 8-MQB molecule is harnessed to develop a fluorescent probe capable of detecting Fe3+ in water samples. The fluorescence intensity exhibits a robust linear correlation with Fe3+ concentrations within the range of 5.0 μM to 0.3 mM. Moreover, the probe demonstrates exceptional resistance to interference from other metal ions. In conclusion, this research presents a universal strategy for designing AIE molecules and introduces an AIE probe for the rapid detection of Fe3+ concentrations in water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron (Fe3+) is ubiquitously distributed in living organisms and serves as an essential trace element vital for sustaining fundamental life processes. Apart from its foundational role in cell pigments and enzymes, Fe3+ actively engages in diverse life processes, encompassing oxidation, enzyme-catalyzed reactions, and cellular metabolism [1]. Precise regulation of its concentration within specific parameters is imperative to ensure the normal function of life. Imbalances in Fe3+ levels can give rise to various health issues, including iron-deficiency anemia and diseases such as cancer [2]. Presently, techniques like spectrophotometry, inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), and atomic absorption spectroscopy are utilized to quantify Fe3+ content in samples. However, these methodologies frequently necessitate costly equipment and intricate sample pre-treatments. Consequently, the development of an affordable, facile, and maneuverable detection method for gauging Fe3+ concentrations in the environment holds substantial significance. Fluorescence method with its merits of rapid detection, high sensitivity, and low detection limits, have been emerged as a pivotal approach for gauging Fe3+ concentrations through coordination effect [3].

Owing to the exceptional coordination capability of the nitrogen atom in quinoline, its Lewis basicity can be adjusted by introducing various functional groups onto quinoline, altering its coordination ability. This modulation facilitates selective coordination, enabling highly specific detection of various metal ions [4–6]. Currently, numerous metal ion detection probes with quinoline as the framework have been developed. However, following coordination with metal ions, the majority of fluorescent probes have a tendency to aggregate into nanoparticles. The presence of the ACQ effect in fluorescent molecules leads to a reduction in the fluorescence intensity of the complexes. The existence of the ACQ phenomenon restricts the application range of fluorescent probes. In 2001, Tang's research group proposed the concept of AIE [7]. The emergence of AIE molecules overcomes the limitations associated with traditional fluorescent probes, providing a novel perspective for the design of fluorescent probes [8,9]. Currently, the design and synthesis of AIE fluorescent molecules for various analytical detection applications are a research hotspot. Initially, researchers introduced molecules with AIE properties, such as tetraphenylethylene (TPE) and triphenylamine (TPA), into the frameworks of traditional fluorescent probes, leading to the design and synthesis of a series of fluorescent probes with AIE characteristics [10,11]. However, with the development of AIE theory, researchers are no longer satisfied with assembling molecules to construct AIE molecules. An increasing number of researchers are approaching the construction of AIE molecules from the perspective of molecular electronic structure, regulating excited-state electronic structure through functional groups. This includes the design of molecules with excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) or twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT) [12–14]. These strategies are applied to enhance the non-radiative transition proportion in solution, leading to fluorescence quenching. However, when forming aggregates, the effective suppression of these non-radiative transitions through intra-molecular interactions within the molecules occurs, resulting in the manifestation of the AIE phenomenon.

Modulating transitions involving (n, π*) orbitals in the excited-state electron configuration is a pivotal approach in the development of versatile organic luminescent materials. This modulation significantly enhances spin-orbit coupling (SOC) and accelerates the conversion of organic molecules from singlet (Sn) to triplet states (Tn), in accordance with the El-Sayed [15]. Numerous studies have focused on constructing a series of organic molecules with room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) or thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) by introducing carbonyl, halogen, and aromatic heterocyclic moieties involving molecular (n, π*) transitions [16–19]. Simultaneously, (n, π*) transitions lead to a substantial increase in the proportion of non-radiative transitions within the excited molecular state, causing quenched fluorescence, which is a valuable tool in the design of AIE molecules [20,21]. When the energy levels of (n, π*) and (π, π*) transitions within a molecule closely align, they can trigger fluorescence deactivation through two distinct mechanisms (Scheme 1 A). Firstly, when the orbital energies of these two states closely match, their vibrational coupling usually initiates a highly efficient internal transition and non-radiative decay, leading to fluorescence quenching, commonly referred to as the proximity effect (PE) [22,23]. The second mechanism involves nonradiative decay through relaxation to the (n, π*) state, facilitated by an almost barrier-free conical intersection (CI) connecting the (π, π*) and (n, π*) states [24,25]. The development of AIEgens through the fine-tuning of the energy distribution of molecular (n, π*) states via changed substituent group is still in its nascent state and presents a substantial challenge.

The excited states of quinoline molecules manifest in two distinct forms: the (π, π*) transition within the quinoline framework and the (n, π*) transition induced by the nitrogen atom in quinoline. The dark-state transition originating from the nitrogen atom in quinoline hampers fluorescence emission, offering potential for the design of AIE molecules. Furthermore, coordination of metal ions with the nitrogen atom effectively suppresses the dark-state transition, rendering it suitable as an AIE probe for metal ion detection. In line with this methodology, diverse substituents are introduced at the 8th position of quinoline in this study to modulate the excited-state electronic structure of quinoline derivatives. Experimental results illustrate that as substituents shift from electron-donating groups (EDG) to electron-withdrawing groups (EWG), quinoline derivatives undergo a transition from ACQ to AIE. The variations in the photophysical properties of these quinoline derivatives are elucidated through transient and steady-state spectra, single-crystal X-ray diffraction (SCXRD), and Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations. The experimental data indicate that the electronegativity of the substituents influences the electronic transition of quinoline derivatives. As substituents shift from EDG to EWG, the excited-state electronic structure of quinoline derivatives shifts from (π, π*) to (n+π, π*), with the presence of the excited state (n, π*) being the primary factor in exhibiting AIE properties in these derivatives. The study systematically investigates the relationship between substituent electronic effects and quinoline (n, π*) transitions, proposing a universal strategy for designing AIE quinoline molecules. Additionally, leveraging the property of the quinoline nitrogen atom in 8-MQB to suppress (n, π*), 8-MQB is employed as a fluorescent probe for detecting Fe3+ in water samples.

All chemicals and reagents were procured from Aladdin Company. The ultimate products utilized in all experiments underwent purification using a silica gel column and were recrystallized at least twice. Doubly distilled water was used throughout the experiments. 1H, 13C NMR spectra were recorded on AVANCE DRX 400 spectrometer (Bruker, German). HPLC-MS were measured by electrospray ionization mass spectra with a High Performance 1100 Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). A C18 column (250 mm* 4.6 mm, 5 μm, Hypersil GOLD, USA) was deployed as stationary phase. Single-crystal X-ray diffraction (SC-XRD) data were collected on a Rigaku Oxford Diffraction Supernova with Atlas Diffractometer, and crystal structures were solved with Olex2 [26]. Fluorescence spectra in the range of 300–700 nm were recorded with the F-4700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan) at room temperature. Fluorescence decay curves measured by 285 nm and 390 nm excitation from Nano LED lamp were obtained on Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer. Fluorescence spectra, phosphorescence spectra and fluorescence quantum yields (QY) were measured on Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer. The size of nanoparticles in the solution was determined by NanoBrook Omni (Bruker, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photophysical properties of quinoline derivatives in solution
ce:section.ce:para: Comprehensive synthesis process details are available in the Supplementary Information (SI). In brief, compounds 8-MQB, 4-(quinolin-8-yl) phenyl acetate (8-QPA), 8-(4-methoxyphenyl) quinoline (8-MPQ), 4-(quinolin-8-yl) benzonitrile (8-QBZ), and 4-(quinolin-8-yl) phenol (8-QLP) were synthesized through a one-step Suzuki coupling reaction, while 8-QBA was derived from the hydrolysis of 8-MQB. The resultant products were purified through column chromatography and were characterized by 1H and 13C NMR spectroscopy as well as high-resolution mass spectrometry (Figs. S1–12).

The photophysical properties of four quinoline derivatives were initially examined in tetrahydrofuran (THF) solution. The maximum absorption wavelengths for carboxyl-substituted 8-QBA and ester-substituted 8-MQB were both observed at 294 nm (Fig. S13 A and B), while those for 8-QPA and 8-MPQ with acetoxy and acetyl substitutions were located at 297 nm and 317 nm in THF solution (Fig. S13C and D), respectively. Additionally, as the substituents changed from EWG to EDG, the maximum absorption wavelengths red-shifted, indicating that the EDG groups contribute to lowering the energies gap between the ground state and the excited state. To investigate the AIE behaviors of these compounds, the poor solvent (water) was gradually introduced into their THF solutions, and their fluorescence emission spectra were measured at their respective maximum excitation wavelengths. The experimental findings reveal that 8-QBA and 8-MQB, substituted with EWG, manifest AIE characteristics, whereas 8-QPA and 8-MPQ, substituted with EDG, demonstrate ACQ properties. Specifically, as the proportion of the poor solvent increases, the fluorescence emission of 8-QBA and 8-MQB gradually rises at 446 nm and 447 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 A and B). In pure THF, the emission peak of 8-QPA is situated at 436 nm (QY = 5.4 %). As the fraction of water (f
w ) increases to 10 %, it shifts to 440 nm (QY = 3.5 %) (Fig. 1C). A further increase in f
w results in a rapid decline in fluorescence intensity at 440 nm. Likewise, the methoxy-substituted 8-MPQ molecule demonstrates analogous fluorescence behavior. With increasing f
w , its emission peak shifts from 413 nm to 437 nm, followed by a swift decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 1D). The gradual addition of water to THF heightens solvent polarity, promoting charge transfer in 8-QPA and 8-MPQ molecules, leading to an initial redshift in their fluorescence spectra [21,27]. Nevertheless, with a subsequent increase in f
w , the formation of aggregates in the solution causes them to exhibit ACQ properties (see Fig. 2).

To investigate whether the formation of nanoparticles influences the spectra of these derivatives, the analysis of the particle size distributions and UV–visible (UV–vis) spectra of the solutions at various f w were conducted. As f w increased, the nanoparticle size of the four quinoline derivatives solutions exhibited distinct degrees of enlargement (Tables S1–4), and the UV–vis spectra of the four derivatives displayed a baseline elevation phenomenon (Fig. S13 A-D). This phenomenon indicated that the forming of nanoparticles was the primary factor driving changes in the molecular spectra. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetimes and quantum yield of the quinoline derivatives solutions under different f w conditions were determined, allowing for the calculation of their radiative (k
r ) and nonradiative (k
nr ) decay rate constants. It was observed that molecules with AIE properties (8-QBA and 8-MQB) displayed an increasing trend in fluorescence lifetime and fluorescence quantum yield as f w increased (Fig. S26 A and B; Tables S8 and S9). Furthermore, the formation of nanoparticles effectively constrained nonradiative decay processes, resulting in a notable decrease in their nonradiative decay rate constants and a concurrent increase in radiative decay rate constants (Tables S8 and S9). Conversely, 8-QPA and 8-MPQ with ACQ properties exhibited the opposite trend. With increasing f w, their fluorescence lifetimes and fluorescence quantum yields decreased, accompanied by a decrease in radiative decay rate constants and an increase in nonradiative decay rate constants (Fig. S26C and D; Tables S10 and S11). These observations confirmed that the formation of nanoparticles amplified the proportion of nonradiative decay processes in these molecules, which is in accordance with their ACQ characteristics.

To investigate the origins of variations in the photophysical properties of these molecules, the electronic structures of the excited state minima (S1, min) for these four quinoline derivatives were initially optimized and followed by natural transition orbital (NTO) analysis using the Multiwfn program [28]. Through the analysis of novel organic molecules, it has been observed that molecules with AIE characteristics, such as 8-QBA and 8-MQB, display significantly different excited-state electronic structures compared to those exhibiting ACQ properties, specifically 8-QPA and 8-MPQ. To delve deeper into the reasons behind these differences in excited-state electronic structures, an examination of the S1 molecular orbital (MO) compositions of these molecules was undertaken. Computational studies have shown that AIE-active molecules have unique S1 molecular orbital compositions in contrast to their ACQ counterparts. Notably, the S1 → S0 transitions in 8-QBA and 8-MQB are characterized by a lower percentage of HOMO → LUMO transition contributions, amounting to 28.9 % and 29.4 % respectively (Table S18). Additionally, the transitions from HOMO-1 → LUMO play a significant role in their S1 →S0 transitions, contributing to 52.9 % and 52.3 %, respectively (Table S18). On the other hand, the S1 →S0 transitions in 8-QPA and 8-MPQ are predominantly comprised of HOMO → LUMO transitions, which account for 96.8 % and 96.7 %, respectively (Table S18). These variations in the electronic structures of the excited states may account for the differing photophysical behaviors observed among these compounds.

To investigate whether the AIE characteristics of 8-QBA and 8-MQB originate from the HOMO-1→LUMO transition, an initial examination of their HOMO-1 orbital structures was conducted. The calculation results demonstrated that the HOMO-1 orbitals are predominantly shaped by the presence of lone pair electrons located on the quinoline N atoms (Fig. S32). The HOMO-1 → LUMO transition is primarily comprised of (n, π*) transitions and driven by the lone pair electrons on the quinoline N atoms. Since (n, π*) transitions can enhance SOC, it is hypothesized that the presence of (n, π*) transitions in the solution state can trigger the transition of 8-QBA and 8-MQB into the triplet state via SOC. Consequently, these highly reactive triplet molecules exchange energy with solvent molecules within the solution, leading to the deactivation of triplet excitons, which results in the quenching of their fluorescence in the solution [29]. Conversely, the absence of (n, π*) transitions in 8-QPA and 8-MPQ effectively hinders their transition from the singlet state to the triplet state, so that the excited-state molecules are deactivated, enabling them to exhibit fluorescence in the solution.

To substantiate the hypothesis, thin film samples of the four derivatives were prepared and doped with a concentration at 0.1 wt% in polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA). The choice of this low doping concentration primarily aimed to maintain the molecules in the monomer state while preventing their interaction with other molecules, thereby avoiding the quenching of triplet excitons [30,31]. To ascertain their triplet state energies, spectroscopic measurements of the film samples were performed at 77 K under both prompt and delayed modes (0.5 m s delayed time). Regarding the thin films of 8-QBA@PMMA and 8-MQB@PMMA in the prompt mode, they displayed emission peaks at 457 nm and 425 nm, respectively, while both molecules exhibited two emission peaks in the delayed mode. Specifically, 8-QBA@PMMA emitted at 457 nm and 480 nm, and 8-MQB@PMMA emitted at 425 nm and 454 nm (Fig. S16 A and B). Furthermore, the corresponding lifetimes for these emission peaks were determined. 8-QBA@PMMA exhibited a short lifetime of 26.23 ns at 457 nm, while it displayed long lifetimes of 3.36 μs and 4.36 μs at 457 nm and 480 nm, categorizing them as phosphorescence emissions (Fig. S20). Similarly, 8-MQB@PMMA demonstrated a short lifetime of 10.62 ns at 425 nm and long lifetimes of 6.31 μs and 7.31 μs at 425 nm and 454 nm, respectively (Fig. S21). These observations indicate that the short-wavelength emission of 8-QBA@PMMA and 8-MQB@PMMA in the delayed mode corresponds to that in the prompt mode, suggesting the possibility of delayed fluorescence, while the longer-wavelength emission aligns with their phosphorescent emission peak. The obtained data in prompt mode revealed that thin films of 8-QPA@PMMA and 8-MPQ@PMMA emitted peaks at 439 nm and 405 nm (Fig. S16C and D). In the delayed mode, these films exhibited weaker emission peaks at 495 nm and 540 nm, respectively. The emission peaks at 439 nm of 8-QPA@PMMA and 405 nm of 8-MPQ@PMMA displayed lifetimes of 13.05 ns and 4.01 ns, categorizing them as fluorescence emissions. Correspondingly, emission peaks at 495 nm of 8-QPA@PMMA and 540 nm of 8-MPQ@PMMA showed lifetimes of 3.68 μs and 2.85 μs, classifying them as phosphorescence emissions (Figs. S22 and S23). The data above suggests that the thin films of 8-QPA and 8-MPQ may not possess the TADF properties.

In order to evaluate the possible TADF characteristics of these molecular film materials, their spectral properties were conducted across a range of temperatures. The results unveil distinct temperature-dependent trends in the spectral behavior of 8-QPA@PMMA and 8-MPQ@PMMA. As temperature increasing, both manifest a reduction in both fluorescence intensity and fluorescence lifetime were observed. This decline can be ascribed to heightened molecular vibrations induced by elevated temperatures, giving birth to an increased prevalence of non-radiative transitions, ultimately leading to a diminishment in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S18; Figs. S22 and S23). The results align with our hypothesis that the thin films of 8-QPA and 8-MPQ lack TADF properties. Turning attention to 8-QBA@PMMA, its fluorescence response to temperature variations can be delineated into three distinct stages. Within the temperature range of 77–257 K, both fluorescence intensity and fluorescence lifetime decreased with increasing temperature. This decrease is the consequence of the intensified thermal vibrations of the molecules as temperature rises, leading to a higher occurrence of non-radiative transitions. Within the temperature range of 257–347 K, an enhancement in fluorescence intensity, fluorescence lifetime, and delayed fluorescence lifetime was observed in the thin film. This phenomenon is consistent with Thermally Activated Delayed Fluorescence (TADF), wherein the temperature rise enables molecules to absorb energy from the surrounding environment to compensate for the energy gap between the excited triplet state and singlet state. Consequently, they can return to the S1 state via reverse intersystem crossing, thus exhibiting the aforementioned photophysical changes. However, when the temperature reaches 377 K, the further intensification of molecular thermal vibrations offset the ability of the RISC process to counteract non-radiative transitions, resulting in a decline in these photophysical parameters (Fig. S18, Fig. S20) [32]. The photophysical behavior of 8-MQB@PMMA followed a similar trend. Within the temperature range of 77–227 K, there was a decrease in fluorescence intensity, fluorescence lifetime, and delayed fluorescence lifetime. As the temperature ascended to the range of 257–317 K, these photophysical parameters exhibited an upward trend for the most part. However, with a further increase in temperature, a reduction in fluorescence intensity and lifetime became evident (Fig. S18, Fig. S21). These empirical findings undeniably confirm that 8-QBA and 8-MQB films do indeed possess the characteristics of TADF.

The hypothesis regarding the AIE mechanism was supported by the photophysical data acquired for these molecules within PMMA thin films. Specifically, 8-QBA and 8-MQB displayed ΔE
ST values of 0.13 eV and 0.18 eV. In contrast, 8-QPA, and 8-MPQ exhibited larger ΔE
ST values of 0.31 eV and 0.76 eV, respectively. The smaller ΔE
ST , in combination with the (n, π*) transition, facilitates the transition of molecules to the excited triplet state. Therefore, after absorbing photons and transitioning to the excited singlet state, 8-QBA and 8-MQB undergo intersystem crossing to convert into excited triplet state molecules. The energy exchange between the triplet state molecules and solvent molecules leads to fluorescence quenching in solution. Upon the introduction of the poor solvent, nanoparticles effectively impede energy exchange among solvent molecules, thus exhibiting AIE. Owing to their substantial ΔE ST values and the absence of (n, π*) transitions, both 8-QPA and 8-MPQ molecules effectively impede the transition from the excited singlet to the triplet state. Consequently, they are capable of returning to the ground state from the singlet state through the emission of photons from the excited singlet state. Fluorescence quenching may arise from intermolecular interactions during the introduction of poor solvents to form nanoparticles. Theoretical calculations of SOC and k
risc effectively validate our conclusions. Specifically, molecules with (n, π*) transition properties, such as 8-QBA and 8-MQB, exhibit significant SOC values of 6.41 cm−1 and 6.39 cm−1, respectively. Conversely, molecules without (n, π*) transitions, such as 8-QPA and 8-MPQ, present smaller SOC values of 0.65 cm−1 and 0.45 cm−1, respectively, effectively inhibiting their conversion from singlet to triplet states in solution. Furthermore, 8-QBA and 8-MQB demonstrate significant Krisc values of 2.02 × 105 and 1.64 × 106, respectively, explaining the occurrence of TADF phenomena in their thin films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photophysical properties of quinoline derivatives in solid state
ce:section.ce:para:
The photophysical properties of these derivatives in their solid state were further investigated. Initially, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the powder samples were recorded. 8-QBA, 8-MQB, 8-QPA, and 8-MPQ showed maximum absorption wavelengths at 296 nm, 299 nm, 321 nm, and 340 nm. (Fig. S14). Then, emission spectra were recorded at their respective excitation maxima wavelength. In specific, the 8-MQB, 8-QPA, and 8-MPQ molecules displayed single emission peaks at 411 nm, 395 nm, and 406 nm, respectively. On the contrary, 8-QBA exhibited dual emissions at 456 nm and 480 nm, which was reminiscent of the emission profiles of the delayed emission peaks observed for 8-QBA@PMMA at 77 K (Fig. S15). To investigate the source of the dual emission in the solid state of 8-QBA, delayed spectra (0.5 m s) were collected at room temperature. Remarkably, the emission peaks in the delayed spectra of 8-QBA and 8-MQB molecules closely coincided with those in their prompt spectra (Fig. S15 A and B). Conversely, no discernible data were obtained for the delayed emission of 8-QPA and 8-MPQ molecules. 8-QBA molecule showing lifetimes of 3.98 μs at 456 nm and 6.82 μs at 480 nm (Fig. S20 B), while the excited-state lifetime of 8-MQB was 4.32 μs (Fig. S21 B). These findings suggest that the 8-QBA and 8-MQB solids possess TADF properties, which are related to their unique (n, π*) transitions in the excited-state electronic structure. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes and quantum yield of these powders were determined and compared with those of their respective PMMA films. The experimental results demonstrated that the 8-QBA at powder state presented the highest fluorescence quantum yield at 42.6 %, followed by the 8-MQB at powder state at 13.1 % (Table S11). Meanwhile, the quantum yields of 8-QPA and 8-MPQ at powder state were 1.71 % and 3.71 %. Interestingly, in the thin-film state, the quantum yields for the AIE molecules decreased to 12.4 % and 4.50 %, whereas the fluorescence quantum yields of the molecules with ACQ effects increased to 5.36 % and 6.72 %, respectively. The fluorescence lifetimes of the film samples displayed varying degrees of increase in comparison to the powder samples. An analysis of their k
r and k
nr reveals that, for AIE molecules, the formation of thin-film samples results in a notable reduction in their radiative transition rates, accompanied by a decrease in their non-radiative transition rates, consequently contributing to the observed decrease in fluorescence quantum yields. In contrast, for ACQ molecules, the fabrication of thin-film samples not only diminishes their non-radiative transition rates but also enhances their radiative transition rates. These results imply a potential correlation between the photophysical properties of the molecules in their aggregated states and the specific stacking configurations of their molecules.

To delve into the inherent correlation between the photophysical properties in their aggregated state and molecular stacking, the single-crystal structures of these quinoline derivatives were investigated. Additionally, the distribution of weak interactions within the crystals was analyzed using the Crystal Explorer 17.5 software [33]. It was found that there are weak π-π interactions in the crystal of 8-QBA and the layer-to-layer distance is 3.621 Å. At the same time, the carboxyl group forms a hydrogen bond with the neighboring molecular quinolines N atom, and the bond length is 1.912 Å (Fig. 3 A). These intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions significantly contribute to stabilizing the triplet exciton, triggering off the emission of phosphorescence at 480 nm at room temperature [34–36]. Furthermore, π-π interactions are observed between 8-QMB molecules in their crystal, distinguished by a reduced layer-to-layer distance of 3.449 Å compared to 8-QBA (Fig. 3B). This reduced distance may contribute to an increased inner filter effect, consequently leading to a significantly lower solid-state fluorescence quantum yield compared to the one of 8-QBA. Regarding 8-QPA, there are more prominent intermolecular π-π interactions compared to those observed in 8-QBA, giving rise to a reduced layer-to-layer distance of 3.333 Å (Fig. 3C). The reduced distance enhances the intermolecular internal filtering effect, ultimately causing a decrease in fluorescence quantum yields in the aggregated state, thereby demonstrating the ACQ phenomenon. Conversely, minimal overlap exists between the aromatic rings of 8-MPQ molecules in their crystal, leading to the absence of π-π stacking interactions among them, which decrease in intermolecular overlap reduces the internal filtration effect to some extent. As a result, the solid-state fluorescence quantum yield is improved compared to that of 8-QPA. Hirshfeld surface analysis demonstrates a gradual decrease in intermolecular C⋯C (P
C
⋯
C ) interactions as the substituent shifts from EWG to EDG, accompanied by a general increase in intermolecular H⋯H (P
H
⋯
H ) interactions and C⋯H (P
C
⋯
H ) interactions. Unfortunately, this trend is detrimental to luminescence behaviors at solid-state. Without C⋯C interactions, vibrational induced nonradiative transitions in the excited state of the molecules are not effectively suppressed, contributing to an increased proportion of non-radiative transitions and a reduced fluorescence quantum yield. For 8-MPQ, intermolecular P
C
…
C make up only 2.3 %, while intermolecular P
H
…
H interactions and P
C
…
H interactions constitute 49.0 % and 35.0 %, respectively (Fig. 3D). This configuration does not support the stabilization of luminescence in the aggregated state, resulting 8-MPQ also displays the ACQ effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model creation for AIE molecular design
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the impact of the electronegativity of substituents on the excited-state orbital compositions of these molecules, we calculated their surface electrostatic potentials (ESP) and electric transition dipole moment (EDM) upon excitation. The calculation data revealed a substantial increase in electron density on the quinoline ring as the substituent shifted from EWG to EDG (Fig. 4 ). This increase is attributed to the conjugation effect with lone-pair electrons on the acetoxy and methoxy groups, thereby upgrading the π-electron density in the aryl ring and facilitating the (π, π*) transition. The analysis of EDM revealed a dramatic increasement as the substituent transitioned from EDG to EWG, which was driven by charge transfer within the molecule. As a result, the heightened EDM could promote oscillator strength in the S1-S0 transition, which effectively improves luminescence efficiency in solution [36,37]. In addition, the energy difference between the HOMO-1 and HOMO orbitals of these molecules was also conducted. Additionally, the calculations have unveiled that the inclusion of the EWG tends to decrease the energy level of the HOMO while simultaneously elevating that of the HOMO-1. Consequently, this alteration diminishes the energy gap between these orbitals. Such adjustments potentially enhance the involvement of the HOMO-1 in the transition to the LUMO (Fig. S32). Conversely, the presence of the EDG produces contrasting effects, enlarging the energy gap and thereby influencing the transition contributions differently. Drawing from the aforementioned calculations, it can be inferred that the EDG reduced electron density in the quinoline ring, decreased the EDM, and alters the distribution of orbital energy levels. This promotes (n, π*) transitions in the S1-S0 transition, causing fluorescence quenching in the solution. In contrast, an electron-donating substituent exhibited opposing behavior. These findings established a theoretical framework for the design of quinoline derivates AIEgens.

To evaluate the precision of the theoretical model, cyano groups (EWG) and hydroxyl groups (EDG) were introduced as substituents into quinoline. Similarly, theoretical calculations were conducted to determine their S1-S0 transitions, ESP, EDM, and orbital energy gaps. The molecular orbital composition of the S1 →S0 transition in the cyano-substituted 8-QBZ molecule is akin to that observed in 8-QBA and 8-MQB. Specifically, the contribution of the HOMO → LUMO transition to the S1-S0 transition in 8-QBZ is 25.0 %, while the HOMO-1 → LUMO transitions account for a significant 59.2 % (as shown in Table S18). This similarity in molecular orbital composition suggests that the cyano substitution in 8-QBZ does not significantly alter the fundamental excited-state electronic structure characteristic of AIE-active molecules, maintaining a low contribution from direct HOMO → LUMO transitions and a high contribution from HOMO-1 → LUMO transitions. Additionally, the SOC coefficient and the k
risc were confirmed to be 6.42 cm−1, and 4.29 × 104 S−1 by theoretical calculation (Fig. 5 A). Meanwhile, analyzing its ESP reveals a decrease in π-electron density on the quinoline ring, which was accompanied by a minor EDM of 1.94 debye (Fig. 5 B). In contrast, the hydroxyl-substituted 8-QLP presents a divergent behavior. Its S1-S0 transition predominantly arises from HOMO→LUMO, constituting a substantial 96.9 % of the transitions (Table S18). This transition is along with a minimal SOC of 0.49 cm−1, and a low k
risc of 1.26 × 10−6 S−1. Besides, due to the electron-donating affect of the hydroxyl group through conjugation, the ESP shows a noticeable increase in π-electron density within the quinoline ring, leading to an elevated EDM of 3.11 Debye. These calculation findings align with the theoretical model, verifying that 8-QBZ may potentially display AIE behavior through the same mechanism, while 8-QLP is expected to perform ACQ properties.

To validate the theoretical accuracy, 8-QBZ and 8-QLP molecules were synthesized by the Suzuki reaction and purified, and their photophysical analyses were measured under various conditions. The synthesized powders of 8-QBZ and 8-QLP exhibited absorption peaks at 309 nm and 342 nm (Fig. S14), respectively, corresponding to the absorption induced by the (π, π*) transition. Emission spectra were recorded in both prompt and delayed modes for these molecules. The 8-QBZ molecule displayed emission peaks at 400 nm in both prompt and delayed modes (Fig. S17 A) with the excited state lifetimes of 4.46 ns and 4.43 μs (Fig. S24 B), respectively, indicating its possession of TADF property. The PMMA film containing the 8-QBZ molecule was prepared, and its spectral data in both prompt and delayed modes at 77 K were also studied. The results revealed that 8-QBZ@PMMA emitted a broad peak at around 420 nm in the prompt mode at 77 K with a measured lifetime of 6.26 ns. In the delayed modes, two emission peaks emerged at 430 nm and 465 nm with lifetimes of 6.26 μs and 8.14 μs (Fig. S17 B), respectively (Fig. S24 B). The ΔE
ST of 8-QBZ in the single-molecule state was calculated to be 0.22 eV based on the spectral data obtained 77 K. Subsequently, spectral and excited state lifetime measurements of 8-QBZ@PMMA were conducted at different temperatures, revealing trends similar to those observed for 8-QBA@PMMA. In short, the fluorescence intensity, fluorescence lifetime, and delayed lifetime of 8-QBZ@PMMA exhibited a decreasing trend in the temperature range of 77–227 K. Remarkably, they showed a marked increase in the temperature range of 257–347 K. However, these photophysical parameters show varying degrees of decrease as the temperature further rose to 377 K. In contrast, 8-QLP powders did not emit detectable fluorescence at the same condition. Intriguingly, when incorporated into PMMA films, 8-QLP exhibited fluorescence emission at 411 nm with a fluorescence quantum yield of 9.3 %. 8-QLP@PMMA at 77 K displayed fluorescence emission peaks at 408 nm and phosphorescence emission peaks at 531 nm with lifetimes of 9.81 ns and 3.21 μs, respectively. The ΔE
ST calculated from the experimental data was 0.7 eV. Fluorescence spectra and lifetimes of 8-QLP@PMMA were determined at different temperatures. Similar to those of 8-QPA@PMMA, with both fluorescence intensity and lifetime were decreased with increasing temperature. To investigate their AIE properties, the fluorescence spectra of 8-QBZ and 8-QLP molecules were measured in THF solution with different f
w . Surprisingly, 8-QBZ did not show detectable fluorescence emission in the THF solution. However, as fw increasing, it gradually displayed fluorescence emission at 443 nm and reached a maximum value at f
w = 90 % (Fig. 5C), proving obvious AIE properties. On the contrary, the 8-QLP molecule exhibited strong fluorescence emission at 433 nm in a pure THF solution (QY = 35.4 %), but its fluorescence intensity was rapidly decreased with the increasing fw with a fluorescence quantum yield of only 3.8 % at f
w = 20 % (Fig. 5 D), suggesting the ACQ behavior.

These results confirm the validity of the proposed AIE mechanism and its applicability to the AIE model. Following this, an investigation into the crystal structures of 8-QBZ and 8-QLP was carried out. Similar to other AIE molecules, the crystal of 8-QBZ displays intermolecular π-π stacking with a substantial layer-to-layer distance of 3.652 Å and a primary proportion of intermolecular P
C
…
C interactions, constituting 15.9 % of the structure. In contrast, the crystal of 8-QLP exhibits pronounced π-π stacking characterized by a reduced layer-to-layer distance of 3.324 Å and a P
C
…
C ratio of 7.4 %, which are responsible for the fluorescence quenching in the solid state.

Iron (III) ion is an important contributor to water pollution. Elevated levels of Fe3+ in aquatic environments pose health risks to both humans and animals. Consequently, numerous fluorescent probes have been developed to detect Fe3+ in water [38,39]. In previous research, it was demonstrated that quinoline N atom (n, π*) transitions, leading to an increase in the proportion of non-radiative transitions, played a key role in initiating the AIE phenomenon within these molecules. Additionally, the lone pair of electrons were responsible for the quinoline N atom presents Lewis basicity. When interacting with a Lewis acid, it effectively suppresses (n, π*) transitions, thereby enabling fluorescence activation. Metal ions are renowned for their strong Lewis acid properties, and the chelation of quinoline N atoms with metal ions may reduce their solubility, resulting in nanoparticle precipitation and a consequent enhancement of AIE molecule fluorescence. Given that compound 8-MQB displayed AIE behavior among these compounds and demonstrates excellent solubility in THF, it has been selected as the fluorescent probe for the detection of metal ions.

To assess the viability of the proposed scheme, twelve metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cd2+, K+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Mn2+) at a concentration of 0.1 mM were introduced into the 0.1 mM solution of 8-MQB in Tris-HCl buffer (f
w = 80 %, pH 4) and the fluorescence spectra of these mixtures were recorded. The experimental findings revealed that the 8-MQB molecule exhibited remarkable selectivity towards Fe3+, and the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm increased with 1.24 times compared to its original value upon the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. 6 A). Furthermore, the vulnerability of 8-MQB to interference was also evaluated by introducing a 0.1 mM Fe3+ solution as a blank and adding other metal ions at concentrations of 1.0 mM into the 8-MQB solution. The results showed that 8-MQB could effectively withstand interference during the detection of Fe3+ (Fig. 6B). Hence, the fluorescence emission spectra of 8-MQB dispersions were recorded in the presence of varying FeCl3 concentrations, and a linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm and the concentration of Fe3+ was established. The data showed a robust empirical relationship between the natural logarithm of Fe3+ concentration (lnC) and fluorescence intensity at 480 nm. The linear range for Fe3+ detection extended from 5.0 μM to 0.3 mM (Fig. 6C and D), and the detection limit was determined to be 3.0 μM based on S/N = 3. Finally, 8-MQB was utilized as a fluorescence probe to detect Fe3+ in real water sample collected from Cuihu Lake in Kunming city, and the concentration of Fe3+ was found to be 0.23 mM. Furthermore, recovery experiments were performed, revealing recoveries ranging from 97.20 % to 103.50 % for different standard samples in Cuihu Lake water. Meanwhile, the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were within the range of 1.5 %–2.6 % (Table S16). These findings emphasize the practical applicability of the 8-MQB fluorescent probe for the determination of Fe3+.

The detection mechanism of 8-MQB to Fe3+ was explored using UV–vis spectra, analysis of nanoparticle size distribution, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Firstly, UV–vis spectra of 8-MQB were recorded after adding varying concentrations of Fe3+. The absorption peak at 294 nm was intensified, accompanied by a raised UV–vis baseline when 8-MQB bound to Fe3+ (Fig. S28), which indicates the formation of nanoparticles. Then, particle size distribution was determined through laser particle sizer measurements. The result showed that the addition of Fe3+ resulted in the increase of particle size from 414.3 nm to 583.4 nm of the 8-MQB mixture (Table S7). Moreover, the XPS data acquired both before and after the interaction between 8-MQB molecules and Fe3+, indicated that the addition of Fe3+ had a negligible effect on the binding energy of C and O (Figs. S29 and S30). Nevertheless, notable changes were observed in the N 1s spectrum, characterized by the emergence of a new peak at 401 eV (Fig. S31 A). This new peak can be ascribed to the increased N 1s binding energy of quinoline N atoms after coordinating with Fe3+ [40,41]. Furthermore, morphological alterations before and after the introduction of Fe3+ were examined by TEM. Before the introduction of Fe3+, 8-MQB displayed a dispersed nanosheet morphology. However, the introduction of Fe3+ led 8-MQB self-assembling with Fe3+ to form nanospheres (Fig. S31 B). The formation of larger nanoparticles through coordination suppressed the nonradiative processes of 8-MQB, which contributed to considerable improvements in both fluorescence lifetime and fluorescence quantum yield. Analysis of the rate constants k
r and k
nr showed that the presence of Fe3+ effectively reduced k
nr while elevating k
r (Table S15). These results collectively indicate that the strong selectivity of 8-MQB for Fe3+ detection arises from the coordination of Fe3+, and hinders the nonradiative transition associated with the quinoline (n, π*) configuration, leading to enhanced fluorescence.

Transitioning quinoline derivatives from ACQ to AIE was successfully achieved by modulating excited-state electron configuration through changing electronegativity of substituents. The inclusion of electron-deficient substituents can alters the distribution of orbital energy levels. This promotes (n, π*) transitions in the S1-S0 transition. Consequently, the increased proportion of non-radiative transitions gave birth to the emergence of the AIE behavior. In accordance with these results, a model for the design of quinoline derivated AIEgens was proposed and was validated by the design of the control molecules. This model presents an innovative approach to design and synthesize AIEgens. At last, as leveraging the coordination of Fe3+ with quinoline N atoms to suppress nonradiative transitions arising from (n, π*) transitions, 8-MQB was developed as an efficient fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+. Satisfyingly, this probe has been successfully utilized to quantify the concentration of Fe3+ in real samples.

Longjie Wang: Writing – original draft. Yuchen Zhang: Methodology. Yibo Chen: Investigation. Xiangdi Huang: Resources. Mingxia Feng: Validation. Zhigang Ma: Visualization. Yanxiong Liu: Formal analysis. Linlin Chen: Data curation. Liyan Zheng: Writing – review & editing. Qiue Cao: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: National Natural Science Foundation of China (21964020 and 22164020), “Ten Thousand Talents Plan” Top Young Talents of Yunnan Province (C6193044), and Yunnan Natural Science Foundation (K264202210049), Science and Technology Program of Quanzhou (2022N011S), The authors thank the Advanced Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University for the sample testing service.
